coredata.dc:title: Cancer cells targeted visible light and alkaline Phosphatase-Responsive TiO2/Cu2+ carbon Dots-Coated wireless electrochemical biosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Giang, Nguyen Ngan
Won, Hyun Jeong
Lee, Gibaek
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of visible light and pyrophosphates (PPi) accompanied by alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity-responsive cancer cells-targeting carbon dots using carbonized hyaluronic acid (CD(HA)) by means of electrochemical signals generated from CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces was investigated. The stimuli-responsive surface provided an effective method for wireless sensors under intracellular conditions with an antifouling effect to visualize the cell adhesion interaction with the surface as well as the detection of cancer cells. The highly distinctive intracellular PPi and ALP levels of cancer cells compared to normal cells and CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis by the CD(HA) chain into cancer cells induced an electrochemical signal change in cell-cultured biosensors detected by electrochemical methods including EIS and a wireless sensing system. The antifouling properties of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ verified by the time-dependent cell detachment occurring on the modified surfaces in the presence of visible light were employed for the detection of cancer. The resistance of cancer cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors considerably increased (248 kΩ) as detachment time triggered by visible light irradiation increased compared to normal cells (152 kΩ), which was ascribed to the dissociation of Cu2+ in the system by PPi and ALP in cancer cells and the cellular internalization of CD(HA). With high sensitivity and accuracy for cancer detection by the visible light and enzyme-responsiveness with low LOD value (2.31 cells/mL) on basis of electrochemical approach and 70.05 cells/mL for optical approach, the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ wireless biosensor offers a promising approach that facilitates cancer diagnosis with antifouling effects to investigate the interaction between cells and modified surfaces.

Tumor targeting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of a) UV–Vis spectra and b) FL intensity of CD(HA), CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+, and CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+. c) Change of FL intensity of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ in the presence of 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, and 1.0 mM of PPi. d) CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ FL intensity and DLS changes with A: CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+, B: CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ + 1 mM PPi + 50 U/L ALP, C: CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ + 1 mM PPi + 100 U/L ALP, D: CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ + 1 mM PPi + 250 U/L ALP, E: CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ + 1 mM PPi + 500 U/L ALP, F: CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ + 1 mM PPi + 1000 U/L ALP; the treatment of PPi and ALP was conducted at the same time. e) SEM image and element mapping of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+.

Changes in resistances of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated substrates after treatment with 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, and 1.0 mM of PPi for a) EIS profile and b) wireless signals and LED experiment. Changes in resistances of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated substrates after simultaneous treatment with 1.0 mM PPi and 50, 100, 250, 500, or 1000 U/L of ALP for c) EIS profile and d) wireless signals and LED experiment.

a-b) Confocal laser scanning microscopy images for antifouling studies and nanoparticle uptake of a) MDCK and b) HeLa cells cultured on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces detached by visible light irradiation for 0, 1, 2, and 3 h. c-d) EIS studies for c) MDCK and d) HeLa cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated Si surfaces detached by visible light irradiation after 0, 1, 2, and 3 h. e-f) Wireless resistance signals and LED experiments using e) MDCK and f) HeLa cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces after 0, 1, and 3 h detachment by visible light irradiation (MCDK and HeLa cell concetration was 105 cells/mL. Scale bar: 100 µm.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of a) MDCK and b) HeLa cells with concentrations of 102, 103, 104, and 105 cells/mL cultured on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces before and after detachment by visible light irradiation for 3 h. Electrochemical studies of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces cultured with cell concentrations of 101, 102, 103, 104, or 105 cells/mL. c-d) EIS studies with c) MDCK and d) HeLa cells after detachment by visible light irradiation for 3 h, respectively. e-f) Wireless analysis and LED experiments with e) MDCK cells after irradiation and f) HeLa cells after detachment by visible light irradiation for 3 h, respectively. Scale bar: 100 µm.

SEM images and EDS profile of a) CD(HA)/TiO2/ Cu2+-coated surface, b) MDCK and HeLa cell-detached CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface after 3 h visible light irradiation. XRD spectra of d) CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface, e) MDCK and f) HeLa cell-detached CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface after 3 h visible light irradiation. (MCDK and HeLa cell concentration was 105 cells/mL).

a-b) Flow cytometry analysis of a) MDCK and b) HeLa cells detached from CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces by different visible light irradiation times. There were no cells left after 3 h visible light irradiation with c) relative MFI of MDCK and HeLa cells by visible light irradiation time. d) Live and dead assay of MDCK and HeLa cells after 12 h incubation. e) MTT assay for cell viability after 3 h visible light irradiation. (MCDK and HeLa cell concetration was 105 cells/mL).

Mechanism of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ wireless biosensor for the detection of cancer and antifouling effect. MDCK (normal cells) seeded on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated substrates were irradiated under visible light until cells complete detached, and there were no significant changes in the electrochemical and fluorescence intensity. On the contrary, the HeLa (cancer cells)-detached surface showed an increase in resistance and fluorescence intensity, which could be recorded by an optical microscope and wireless device.

head.ce:title: Cancer cells targeted visible light and alkaline Phosphatase-Responsive TiO2/Cu2+ carbon Dots-Coated wireless electrochemical biosensor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of visible light and pyrophosphates (PPi) accompanied by alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity-responsive cancer cells-targeting carbon dots using carbonized hyaluronic acid (CD(HA)) by means of electrochemical signals generated from CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces was investigated. The stimuli-responsive surface provided an effective method for wireless sensors under intracellular conditions with an antifouling effect to visualize the cell adhesion interaction with the surface as well as the detection of cancer cells. The highly distinctive intracellular PPi and ALP levels of cancer cells compared to normal cells and CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis by the CD(HA) chain into cancer cells induced an electrochemical signal change in cell-cultured biosensors detected by electrochemical methods including EIS and a wireless sensing system. The antifouling properties of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ verified by the time-dependent cell detachment occurring on the modified surfaces in the presence of visible light were employed for the detection of cancer. The resistance of cancer cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors considerably increased (248 kΩ) as detachment time triggered by visible light irradiation increased compared to normal cells (152 kΩ), which was ascribed to the dissociation of Cu2+ in the system by PPi and ALP in cancer cells and the cellular internalization of CD(HA). With high sensitivity and accuracy for cancer detection by the visible light and enzyme-responsiveness with low LOD value (2.31 cells/mL) on basis of electrochemical approach and 70.05 cells/mL for optical approach, the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ wireless biosensor offers a promising approach that facilitates cancer diagnosis with antifouling effects to investigate the interaction between cells and modified surfaces.

ce:section.ce:para: Highly sensitive and selective biosensor systems have been designed for the detection of cancer to overcome the limitations of conventional screening methods [1–3]. Many alterations in intracellular and extracellular environmental factors, such as redox activity, pH, and overexpressed enzyme concentration, have been exploited for stimuli-responsive systems to enhance cancer targeting accuracy [4–7]. Current biosensors are mostly based on optical methods, which possess limitations including low sensitivity, processability, and portability. Therefore, there is an urgent need for an innovative system with high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of cancer. The electrochemical method has gained a lot of attention and been applied in the biological field because of its promising properties such as rapidity, high sensitivity, and selectivity [8–11]. Conventionally, graphene is widely used as a material for electrochemical sensors, although graphene-based materials are limited in functionalization, which makes graphene less responsive to biological factors [12,13]. Recently, sp2 bond-bridged carbon dots (CDs) formed by carbonization of biomaterials with excellent properties such as biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity, coatability, and tunable sensitivity have attracted considerable attention in the fields of bioimaging, medical diagnosis, and catalysis [14–18]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) was found to interact with CD44 receptors overexpressed on the surface of cancer cells; therefore, carbonized CD(HA) could be a potential fluorescent probe and has demonstrated selective targeting in the detection of cancer via the endocytosis process [19–21]. Additionally, via the quenching phenomenon of copper integrated into the CD by FRET, the CD can be utilized as a sensor for intracellular overexpressed PPi and ALP in cancer cells. Cu2+-PPi chelation enables the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs, and ALP cooperatively transfers PPi into Pi via its kinase effect to release Cu2+ that re-quenches the fluorescence of CDs, simultaneously causing conductivity changes of the system [22,23]. Consequently, CDs would be a favorable candidate for electrochemical biosensors that indicate cancer-responsive electrical conductivity based on electron transfer, which supports a high accuracy in cell-surface interaction monitoring.

The antifouling technology of anticancer biosensing systems offers a unique combination of performance capabilities and analytical features. The interaction between cells and substrates of coated sensors can allow for the assessment of cell functions such as cell differentiation, cycle, migration, and survival. The antifouling of the surface is conventionally conducted by trypsinization, and the detached cell activities are investigated by microscopy. However, trypsin induces the dysregulation of cell functions, affecting cell activities as a result of vigorous proteolytic behavior during prolonged incubation [24,25]. Stimuli-responsive-based polymer materials such as thermal, electrical, and pH-responsive materials have been proposed to promote the antifouling effect for harvesting cells from surfaces. However, these conventional methods have limitations that hinder their applicability, including various required reagents, synthesis steps, and homogeneously modified cell growth environment conditions.

Titanium oxide (TiO2)-coated antifouling surfaces exhibit a transition from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic state under UV irradiation, which results in dysregulation of sterol production influencing cell membranes, causing alterations in membrane fluidity, permeability, and function [26,27]. Park and co-workers have successfully exploited the great nature of polymer dots in shifting the UV exposure range of TiO2 to visible light by immobilizing TiO2 into a polymer dot system utilizing the up-conversion properties of CDs, which allow adhered cells on coated surfaces to be detached under visible light irradiation [28]. In addition, the study indicated a favorable method for monitoring and harvesting cancer cells, as the procedure is not as complicated as conventional approaches, and maintains cell viability for research purposes, as there has been no report that visible light damages cells.

Herein, we designed a hybridized electrochemical biosensor for the detection of cancer cells in response to intracellular pyrophosphate (PPi) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) with antifouling effects based on visible light by coating CD44-targeted enzyme/visible light-sensitive TiO2/Cu2+ immobilized CD(HA) onto the surface. The proposed stimuli-responsive surface has demonstrated a potent method for cancer diagnosis using resistance changes based on the interaction of ALP-PPi and Cu2+ using cell detachment manipulation on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors with visible light, after which the electrochemical signals could be obtained via a wireless system.

Hyaluronic acid (HA; Mw 200 K), dopamine hydrochloride (DOPA, a catechol derivative), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide, phosphate buffer (PBS), hombikat (anatase titanium oxide), copper chloride (anhydrous), alkaline phosphatate (ALP), pyrophosphates (PPi), Trizma base (TBS), [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (MTT) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Korea. Penicillin–streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% (w/v) trypsin, 0.05% (w/v) EDTA solution, Minimum Essential Media (MEM) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI)-1640 medium were purchased from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Calcein AM and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Invitrogen (USA). HeLa and MDCK cells were obtained from the Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea). Solid substrates: silicon wafer (Si), poly ethylene terephthalate (PET), were prepared, according to the protocols reported in a previous study.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were performed by using an Optizen 2020 UV spectrophotometer (Mecasys Co.). Photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer L550B luminescence spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded using a D-8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Corp). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were attained by an Omicrometer ESCALAB (Omicrometer, Taunusstein, Germany). Particle size was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano, Malvern, Kassel, Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed with a JSM-6700F, JEOL (Japan). The electrochemical sensing was affirmed using electrochemical impedance spectrometer (CS350, CorrTest Instrument, China) and a wireless device consisted of Arduino Uno micro-controller (ATmega328 P Processor) as a sensing part, smartphone as a real-time data display, and bluetooth module (AppGosu). A multimode microplate reader, Filter MaxF3 (Molecular Devices, LLC), was used for the MTT assay. Flow cytometry data (fluorescence-activated cell sorting, FACS) were inscribed using an Attune NxT Acoustic Focusing Cytometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy images were observed by C2 confocal microscope (Nikon, Japan). The water contact angle of polymer dot coated surface was estimated by a DO3210 instrument (KRUSS DSA 100 Optima system, KRUSS, Hamburg, Germany).

CD(HA) were synthesized followed by previous report [29,30]. To prepare the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2
+ , CD(HA) (0.27 g) were dissolved in TBS pH 8.5 (20 mL) and stirred at room temperature. After completely dissolved, TiO2 (0.045 g) and CuCl2 (0.045 g) dissolved in TBS pH 8.5 (10 mL) were simultaneously added into the solution. After a 24 h reaction, the mixture was purified by dialysis with a 1 kD molecular weight cutoff and freeze-dried to obtain the final product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopic studies for CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ with PPi and ALP
ce:section.ce:para: For FL spectroscopy study, CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was dispersed in DDW with a concentration of 1 mg/mL. 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 mM of PPi were added into the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ solution to obtain the recovery of fluorescence, which were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 460 nm.

To investigate the ALP effect in hydrolyzing PPi, ALP at various concentration 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000 U/L were mixed with 1.0 mM PPi and then added into the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL. The fluorescence intensity was then recorded by FL analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle measurements for CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated on various substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was coated on different surfaces (PET, Si wafer) to assess the material-independent surface coating. The substrates were first washed with acetone, ethanol. CDs were dissolved in Tris-buffer solution pH 8.5 at the concentration of 5 mg/mL and using the dip-coating method for substrate-coated. After 12 h, at room temperature, CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated substrates were washed with DDW several times. The contact angles of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated substrates were measured after being dried completely and irradiated under visible light at 0, 1, 6, 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP effect monitoring for aqueous state and coated surfaces of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, HeLa at 1 × 105 cells/mL density were seeded onto a bare substrate and incubated for 12 h with 5% CO2, 37 °C in humidified atmosphere, followed by the incubation of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ 0.1 mg/mL in 1 h and subsequent addition of ALP inhibitor. The HeLa cells treated CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ probe without ALP inhibitor was used as control.

For coated surface states (solid state), HeLa cells with a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL were incubated onto a CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated surface with the reported condition, followed by the addition of ALP inhibitor. The cells were then monitored by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) to observe and confirm the effect of ALP in cancer cells. The HeLa cells cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated surface was used as control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS studies for the detection of cancer cells and antifouling effect
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical signal, the resistance changes of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensor for cancer detection was measured by an electro impedance spectroscopy. The resistance of coated Si wafer substrate was recorded in advance, and subsequently, 1 × 105 cells/mL HeLa and MDCK cells were cultured separately on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated substrates following by 12 h incubation at a condition of the humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2, 37 °C. Finally, the cell solution was removed; the substrates were washed by PBS several times to remove non-attached cells. After being detached by visible irradiation at 0, 1, 3 h, substrates were rinsed with PBS pH 7.4. The electro impedance spectroscopy was used to measure resistance alterations in each step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling study and fluorescence imaging for cancer sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To observe the fluorescence of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ in response to cancer cells, 200 μL HeLa and MDCK cells were distinctly cultured on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated substrates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per mL per well and incubated in 12 h with the condition of the humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2, 37 °C. Subsequently, the cell solution was removed; the substrates were washed by PBS several times to remove non-attached cells. Finally, the surfaces were treated under visible irradiation at 0, 1, 2, 3 h to investigate fluorescent uptake by cancer cells by using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wireless sensor study of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ cancer sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated substrates incubated with HeLa and MDCK cells were connected to the wireless device, which comprised a microcontroller (Arduino Uno) and Bluetooth module (AppGosu). The results were displayed by a smartphone as a resistance graph by the Bluetooth connection between the wireless device and smartphone that activated the wireless system.

Six-well plates were coated with CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ in 10 h, then washed with DDW and PBS thoroughly. After being dried, 2 mL HeLa and MDCK cells were seeded and incubated in a CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ coated six-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per mL per well in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2, 37 °C for 12 h. The cells were collected by detachment by visible irradiation after 0, 1, 3 h and resuspended in 1% FBS in PBS pH 7.4. Trypsinization was conducted to remove cells from the irradiated surfaces. The cell removed by trypsin were resuspended in 1% FBS in PBS pH 7.4. The nanoparticle uptake for trypsinized cells and visible irradiated cells was analyzed by Attune NxT Acoustic Focusing Cytometer at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm.

The biocompatibility of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ and viability of HeLa and MDCK cells after 3 h visible irradiation was investigated by an MTT assay. Mainly, 200 μL of cell solution containing 1 × 105 cells per mL was cultured in a CD(HA)//TiO2/Cu2+ coated 96-well plate and then incubated at 37 °C, 5% CO2 atmosphere for 12 h. The cell-cultured 96-well plate was then detached by 3 h under visible irradiation. Subsequently, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added in each well following by incubation for 4 h. Finally, 200 μL of DMSO was added following by shaking in 15 min. The absorbance was then recorded at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of visible light and enzyme-responsive electrochemical biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , an electrochemical biosensor was fabricated by utilizing visible light and enzyme-responsive CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ on the coated surface in the presence of abnormal concentrations of ALP and PPi in cancer cells. CD(HA) carbonized by HA, which was synthesized following a previously reported method, was simultaneously mixed with CuCl2 and TiO2. The catechol groups in CD(HA) capture Cu2+ and TiO2 to create CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+. The fluorescence of CD(HA) was quenched after the integration of TiO2 and Cu2+, causing an increase in the conductivity of the system in terms of electrochemical properties [31]. CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was subsequently employed as an electrochemical biosensor for the detection of cancer in the presence of ALP and PPi in cancer cells, as shown in the scheme. PPi in cancer cells could form complexes with Cu2+ in the CD(HA), leading to the release of Cu2+ out of the system. PPi was then hydrolyzed by ALP to liberate Cu2+ from the PPi-Cu2+ complex, causing a re-quenching state [32,33]. Notably, the chelation of Cu2+ and PPi could enable the fluorescence of CD(HA) to partially recover in the presence of ALP on solid biosensors, which could be an indicator for the detection of cancer cells. At the same time, the increase in resistance resulted in a decrease in conductivity after Cu2+-PPi chelation, which could be utilized as a signal for electrochemical biosensors in cancer detection. Consequently, a wireless sensing system could be fabricated by using a wireless device by which the signal of resistance changes could be recorded via a smartphone, allowing a simple and rapid method for cancer detection.

To identify the structure of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+, UV–Vis spectroscopy was performed, as shown in Fig. 1
a. Similar spectra peaks at 280 and 360 nm for CD(HA) and CD (HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ represented the conjugation of catechol groups in the structures, which was ascribed to the π-π* of the aromatic rings and the n-π* transition of C = O in catechol groups [34,35]. The overall spectrum of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was up-shifted compared with the spectrum of CD(HA), indicating an increase in the size of the nanoparticles; hence, the fluorescence was quenched by the interaction of CD(HA) and Cu2+. The fluorescence intensity of CD(HA), CD(HA)/TiO2, and CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ is shown in Fig. 1 b. As illustrated in the figure, after the complexation of TiO2 and Cu2+, the size increased from 96.3 to 246.9 nm. Additionally, to verify the complex of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+, XPS analysis was conducted ( Figure S2 ). The Cu2p1/2, Cu2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, Ti2p3/2 signals were detected in the XPS profile of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ besides C1s and O1s at 283.6 and 532.2 eV, respectively, demonstrating the successful integration of TiO2 and Cu2+ into the CD(HA) nanoparticle [36–40]. CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ nanoparticles were treated with 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, and 1.0 mM PPi to evaluate the fluorescent recovery of the nanoparticle following the fluorescence recovery shown in Fig. 1 c. As can be observed in the figure, the addition of PPi resulted in an increase in fluorescence recovery as the amount of PPi added increased, which might be explained by the dissociation of Cu2+ in the nanoparticle. It was observed that after the addition of 1.0 mM of PPi, the fluorescence recovered up to 70% compared with CD(HA)/TiO2. The change in size of the nanoparticles was recorded by DLS to confirm the immobilization of TiO2 and Cu2+ into the structure of CD(HA) (
Figure S3 ). To further investigate the effect of ALP and PPi on size changes of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ nanoparticles, the nanoparticles were simultaneously treated with 1.0 mM PPi at various concentrations (50, 100, 250, 500, and 1000 U/L). As shown in the figure, the size of the nanoparticles slightly decreased at ALP 50 U/L, which confirmed the small amount of disaggregation of Cu2+ out of the nanoparticles at low concentrations. Nevertheless, at higher concentrations of ALP, the size of the nanoparticles remained unchanged at high concentrations, which validated the hydrolysis of PPi to Pi by ALP in solution. The FL intensity analysis was conducted, following the same conditions as DLS analysis, to verify the PPi-ALP effect on the fluorescence of the nanoparticle ( Figure S4 ). As can be observed in the figure, the addition of PPi and ALP together into the nanoprobe at a low concentration of ALP (50 U/L) promoted insignificant fluorescence recovery. However, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased and reached a low intensity almost the same as the fluorescence intensity of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ nanoparticle as the concentration of ALP increased to 1000 U/L, suggesting that PPi is hydrolyzed into Pi by ALP at higher concentrations, which enables Cu2+ to remain in the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ system to maintain its quenching state. Fig. 1 d shows the summarized changes in DLS and FL intensity analysis after cooperative treatment with PPi-ALP. The SEM images showed the round shape of the nanoparticle (Fig. 1 e) as it became aggregated by TiO2 and Cu2+ complexation, proving the successful integration of TiO2 and Cu2+ into the CD system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible light and enzyme-responsive sensing performance of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors
ce:section.ce:para:
To examine the electrochemical and photocatalytic effect on the coating surface under visible irradiation, CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was applied onto polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and silicon wafer substrates by the dip coating method [41,42]. As shown in Figure S5 , the contact angles of the coated substrates declined, indicating the hydrophilicity after prolonged visible light irradiation, which was probably the result of hydroxide species generation in the TiO2 photochemical reaction. To investigate the electrochemical activity of PPi on the nanoparticles, we applied different concentrations of PPi onto CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces (0.05, 0.2, 0.5, and 1.0 mM of PPi) (Fig. 2
a). The signal changes in resistance affected by PPi were obtained by EIS along with a wireless system and LED experiments, which illustrated the increase in resistance with the increase in PPi concentration. This could be explained by the Cu2+ released from CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ via chelation interaction between PPi and Cu2+ on the coated surface, which caused an increase in the resistances and simultaneously declined the power of LED lamps ( Fig. 2 b). Furthermore, considering that the fluorescence recovery of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ by PPi can be inhibited by the dephosphorylation activity of ALP in PPi, which prevents PPi from forming a PPi-Cu2+ complex, resulting in the resistance change and alteration of wireless signals of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface, we simultaneously treated the coated surface with PPi solution (1 mM) and various concentrations of ALP (50, 100, 250, 250, 500, and 1000 U/L). As shown in Fig. 2 c, with the increase in the ALP concentration, the resistance displayed in EIS of the treated surfaces gradually decreased and remained unchanged at 1000 U/L, which prevented the formation of the complex of PPi and Cu2+ as the concentration of ALP was increased. Accompanied by EIS signals, the wireless signals showed a decline in resistance, which enclosed the currents, and intensification of LED increased as the ALP concentration increased.

Additionally, to investigate the kinetic properties of ALP on PPi-treated CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces, EIS analysis of the coated biosensors under the in-turn treatment of ALP at various concentrations, following the treatment of 1 mM PPi onto CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces, was conducted ( Figure S6
a). As observed in Figure S6
a, when treated with PPi, the resistance of the coating increased due to the released Cu2+ by PPi. Meanwhile, there was a slight decrease in resistance values after the subsequent addition of ALP, which verified the PPi-Cu2+ decomplexation caused by ALP dephosphorylation that complexed Cu2+ into the CD system. However, the resistance value decrease was not significant and almost ceased when the concentration of ALP added to the system increased and reached 1000 U/L, which might prove that in the solid state, only a small amount of Cu2+ could return to the CD system. To verify our hypothesis, the fluorescence imaging of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces under the same conditions mentioned above were monitored, as shown in Figure S6
b. Strong fluorescence intensity was observed after treatment with PPi (1 mM), and the fluorescence slightly declined and remained almost unchanged after the addition of increasing concentrations of ALP, whose fluorescence intensity can be compared by depth profile. Therefore, it could be revealed that in the solid state, Cu2+ was able to partially recombine with the CD-coated surfaces, indicating that the interaction of PPi and Cu2+ following ALP addition is nearly irreversible, as proven by the remaining fluorescence intensity on PPi-treated surfaces after increasing ALP addition ( Figure S6
b).

To verify the ALP activity in cancer cells, CLSM imaging of HeLa cells incubated on the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface (solid state CLSM) and HeLa cells on the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ probe (solution state) incubated with an ALP inhibitor was conducted (
Figure S7 ). Briefly, HeLa cells at a density of 105 cells/mL were incubated on a CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface under the reported conditions, followed by the addition of an ALP inhibitor to solid CLSM ( Figure S7
a). In the solution state, HeLa cells with the same density were incubated onto a bare PET surface under the above conditions, followed by incubation with CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ (0.1 mg/mL) for 1 h and subsequent addition of ALP inhibitor ( Figure S7
b). As shown in Figure S7 , the samples treated with ALP inhibitor exhibited obviously higher fluorescence intensity than the samples without ALP inhibitor, which validated that the hydrolysis of PPi to Pi by ALP did not occur because of the inhibition of ALP. Significantly, in solid-state CLSM imaging, the cells without ALP inhibitor treatment showed stronger fluorescence intensity than those in a solution state, which supported our hypothesis that the decomplexation of Cu2+-PPi by ALP was mainly irreversible in the solid state, leading to the uptake of Cu2+ by PPi by cancer cells incubated on coated surfaces. As a result, this phenomenon could be utilized for cancer sensing based on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated biosensors because of the distinctive difference in PPi concentration between cancer and normal cells in the presence of ALP activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wireless electrochemical performance for cancer sensing by antifouling effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The visible light-responsive CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface for antifouling effect was monitored following the 12 h-incubation of HeLa and MDCK cells at a concentration of 105 cells/mL cultured individually on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces. It could be seen from the confocal laser scanning (CLSM) images (Fig. 3
a, b first rows) that after 2 h of exposure to visible light irradiation, ~60% cell fouling was observed, and complete removal was observed after 3 h of irradiation as a result of the hydrophobicity-hydrophilicity transition of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces under visible light due to the narrow bandgap of TiO2-loaded CD(HA). The activity of PPi and ALP levels in the endogenous cellular environment with CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was further investigated by CLSM imaging to determine the fluorescent response of cancer cells by incubation of MDCK and HeLa cells on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces. Fig. 3 a shows the changes in fluorescence prior to visible light irradiation time on the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces. Under CLSM, there was no fluorescence emission of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface detected due to the FRET quenching effect of Cu2+, which served as a control compared to the fluorescent recovery process of cells. The fluorescence of the MDCK cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensor was not detected after the cell seeding process or during the detachment process by visible light irradiation due to its low intracellular PPi concentration. In contrast, the HeLa cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensor showed remarkably stronger fluorescence intensity at 0 h irradiation, as indicated by the Cu2+ captured by a higher amount of PPi in cancer cells. During the detachment process triggered by visible light irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of cancer cells declined following a decrease in the number of cells due to the antifouling effect, while the fluorescence intensity of the surfaces gradually increased. It was hypothesized that Cu2+ was chelated by a high concentration of PPi in cancer cells, causing fluorescent recovery of the surfaces after the detachment of cancer cells.

Electrochemical methods have been reported to be selective and precise detection methods for the detection of cancer cells relying on cell adhesion on modified surfaces, which is based on electrochemical signals generated during performance. Thus, in this study, EIS and wireless sensing assays were conducted to assess the cell-surface interaction leading to the detection of cancer cells through the antifouling effect. EIS analysis of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors cultured with MDCK and HeLa cells, which compared the resistance changes based on the visible light-responsive antifouling effect prior to visible light exposure time, was performed (Fig. 3 c-d). The resistance of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensor was preliminarily measured following the resistance due to MDCK and HeLa cell attachment to the biosensor (46.482 kΩ for the biosensor, 123 kΩ for the biosensor cultured with MDCK cells, and 130 kΩ for the biosensor cultured with HeLa cells). Although the resistance of the biosensors cultured with MDCK and HeLa cells was similar, the resistances of these groups showed distinctive values after the detachment process after different hours of exposure to visible light. The HeLa cell-cultured biosensors exhibited a remarkable increase in the resistance after 1 to 3 h exposure to visible light (147 kΩ and 248 kΩ, respectively), while the MDCK cell-cultured biosensors exhibited gradual changes after 1 h of irradiation, and after 3 h of exposure, there was only a slight change in the resistance of the MDCK-cultured biosensor (152 kΩ). This increase in resistance in HeLa cell-detached coated surfaces might be interpreted by the chelation of Cu2+ in CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ by overexpressing the PPi concentration to form a Cu2+-PPi complex in cancer cells, which caused an increase in the resistance of HeLa cell-cultured biosensors. Subsequently, a wireless sensing system was prepared by arranging CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors with a wireless device and a smartphone, as shown in Fig. 3 e and f. The system showed alterations in the attribution of time-dependent visible light exposure of MDCK and HeLa cells cultured on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors. The phenomenon obtained by the wireless sensing system corresponded to the resistance obtained by EIS analysis. The CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface showed low resistance compared to the cell-cultured coated surface before and after detachment, which was displayed by the low-position resistance line on the screen of the app. After 3 h of exposure to visible light, the HeLa cell-cultured surfaces showed a stronger signal increase in the resistance compared with MDCK cell-cultured surfaces, represented by the high position of the resistance line, which led to a significant decrease in the electrical power of the LED lamp. To verify the interpretation of electrochemical signals obtained after cell detachment by visible light irradiation, we measured the impedances of CD(HA)- and CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces cultured with MDCK and HeLa cells after detachment by trypsin ( Figure S8 ). As shown in Figure S8
c-d, the resistance of the MDCK cell-detached surfaces slightly increased, while there was a dramatic upsurge in the resistance of HeLa cell-detached surfaces, which was likely consistent with the resistance value of the HeLa-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ after detachment by 3 h exposure to visible light irradiation, proving the dissociation of Cu2+ by PPi in cancer cells, causing an increase in resistance after the antifouling effect occurred. Additionally, as observed in Figure S8
a-b, there was enhancement in resistance of HeLa cells cultured on CD(HA)-coated surfaces after trypsinization compared to that of MDCK cells, confirming the endocytosis of CD(HA) penetration through CD44 receptors on cancer cells, which demonstrated that some nanoparticles along with Cu2+ were taken up after the detachment of cancer cells by visible light irradiation. The electrochemical phenomenon manifested the accuracy of the electrochemical signals altered by the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ electrochemical biosensor for the detection of cancer by antifouling in response to visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wireless electrochemical senor of cancer cells detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the mechanism of wireless sensing of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors in response to different amounts of ALP and PPi in cancer and normal cells, the changes in fluorescence and resistance at different cell concentrations and surface morphology after detachment by visible light irradiation were investigated. To monitor the fluorescence emission based on intracellular ALP and PPi activity at different cell concentrations, MDCK and HeLa cells at concentrations of 102, 103, 104, and 105 cells/mL were individually cultured onto CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces for 12 h and detached by 3 h of visible light irradiation as previously investigated to completely remove cells. As shown in Fig. 4
a, no fluorescence was detected on the surfaces cultured with MDCK cells before detachment as well as after detachment of cells, which was displayed by the depth profile with almost no signal appearing. On the contrary, as shown in Fig. 4 b, on the surfaces cultured with HeLa cells, the intensity of the detected fluorescence before the detachment increased as the cell concentration increased, which resulted in increased surface fluorescence after cell detachment by 3 h of visible light irradiation, represented by the intensity signals in depth profiles due to the capture of Cu2+ by PPi with ALP localized in cancer cells that dissociated Cu2+ from the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ causing the fluorescence recovery due to a higher effect of PPi than ALP. The sensitivity of cancer sensing based on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors was further confirmed by EIS analysis of MDCK and HeLa cells after detachment by visible light irradiation for 3 h at different cell concentrations (Fig. 4 c-d). As shown in Figure S9 , the initial resistances of MDCK and HeLa cells at different concentrations were correspondingly similar; however, the resistances of MDCK and HeLa cells after 3 h of visible light irradiation displayed distinctively different signals. As observed in Fig. 4 c-d, after detachment, the resistances of surfaces cultured with MDCK cells (104 and 105 cells/mL) slightly increased compared to the initial one before detachment, while the surfaces cultured with 101 to 103 cells/mL showed a decline in the resistance, which proved that at a low concentration of normal cells, there were almost no interactions between Cu2+ and PPi with ALP in the cellular environment of normal cells. However, the resistance of the surfaces cultured with HeLa cells showed a considerable increase after detachment as the cell concentration increased even with the low concentration of cancer cells at 101, 102, and 103 cells/mL, which elucidated the sensitivity of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ electro biosensors on the basis of cancer cell density. Finally, to observe the sensing ability of the biosensors in terms of electrical signals, we prepared a wireless device connected to CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors cultured with MDCK and HeLa cells at different cell concentrations. The results obtained by the wireless system corresponded to the EIS analysis results. The resistance lines of the HeLa groups increased, indicating an enhancement in resistance by which the power values of LED lamps, respectively, changed, followed by the changes in the resistance. Remarkably different resistance gaps after detachment of HeLa groups could be observed (Fig. 4 e-f). Noticeably, after detachment, the power of LED lamps connected to the surfaces cultured with HeLa cells remarkably declined with the increase in concentration of cancer cells, and the increase in resistance after detachment showed excellent sensitivity by the enzyme response to the concentration of cancer cells to form a PPi-Cu2+ complex that presented selectivity between cancer and normal cells. Furthermore, to verify the efficacy of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ electrochemical biosensor for cancer cells, the limit of detection (LOD) value were determined depending on cell density (from 101 to 105 cells/mL) on basis of electrochemical (resistance value) and optical (fluorescent intensity value) approach. As shown in Figure S10 , the calculated LOD value of electrochemical method was 2.31 cells/mL, while optical based method showed higher value for detection (70.05 cells/mL) which consequently demonstrated the sensitivity of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ electrochemical biosensor toward a low density of cancer cells. The CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensor could be favorable for early detection of cancer cells as the CD(HA) backbone could target effectively CD44 receptors accompanied with ALP-PPi interaction with Cu2+ to prevent the tumor growth led by the prevalent enhancement of CD44/CD24 ratio in cancer cells [43–46].

As shown in Fig. 5 , the surface morphologies of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors (Fig. 5 a), which retained the Cu aggregation, were almost similar to those of the MDCK cell-cultured CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors (Fig. 5 b). However, the HeLa-cultured biosensor exhibited different morphologies due to the dissociation of Cu2+ from CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors by PPi chelation (Fig. 5 c). Elemental analysis by EDX (Fig. 5 a-5c) also demonstrated that Cu2+ dissociation occurred in cancer cell microenvironments (0.39 wt% copper for CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors, 0.39 wt% copper for normal cells, and 0 wt% copper for cancer cells). The XRD analysis shown in Fig. 5 d-f clearly supported the previous study that Cu2+ could only be chelated in a cancerous environment, which verified the composition in each sample [47]. The 2θ values of the peaks in the range of 40–60° and 70° confirmed the integration of Cu2+ into the system (Fig. 5 a), which mostly remained after visible light irradiation of the MDCK cultured biosensor, as shown in Fig. 5 b. In contrast, the XRD profile of the HeLa cell-cultured biosensor did not display any peak value of Cu2+, which reaffirmed the dissociation of Cu2+ in the cancerous environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of normal and cancer cells from CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the cancer-targeting behavior of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ in response to the distinctive intracellular ALP and PPi levels between cancer and normal cells with antifouling effects prior to time-dependent detachment by visible light irradiation, flow cytometry analysis was conducted. After 12 h of incubation, the cells cultured on coated surfaces were detached by exposure to 0, 1, 2, or 3 h visible light, after which the detached cells were collected and analyzed by FACS, and the cells were completely removed after 3 h of visible light irradiation. Simultaneously, the remaining cells on coated surfaces were removed by treatment with trypsin, which was used for FACS analysis to evaluate the cellular uptake of each cell type. As shown in Fig. 6
a and S11a, the cellular uptake of MDCK cell groups did not show a significant increase in intensity when treated with CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+, which could be observed by the change in mean fluorescence values during detached time and after trypsinization. The highest mean fluorescence values of MDCK cells (with the control of 156) after trypsinization and visible light irradiation were 167 and 156, which corresponded to 1.82% and 1.33%, respectively, due to the lower concentration of PPi and ALP. However, the HeLa group treated with CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ (with the control of 154) increased to more than 59.19% by trypsinization and 53% after 3 h of visible light irradiation, represented by the values of 306 and 260, which was far higher than that of the MDCK groups by the PPi-Cu2+ chelation effect (Fig. 6 b,
Figure S11
b). Fig. 6 c and
Figure S11
c illustrate the mean fluorescence intensity of MDCK and HeLa groups in accordance with visible light irradiation and trypsinization on the irradiated surfaces, which could be explained by the Cu2+ uptake by cancer cells due to the dissociation of Cu2+ by overexpressed PPi out of the nanoparticles accompanied by the cellular uptake of CD(HA) nanoparticle CD44 receptors on the cancer cell membrane. The phenomenon exhibited in FACS analysis indicated a remarkable differentiation between the response of the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ nanoparticle to PPi and ALP levels in normal and cancer cellular environments and contemporarily demonstrated the selectivity of cancer targeting through CD44 receptor-mediated nanoparticles. To observe the viability of cells after 12 h incubation on the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface, an in vitro live and dead assay for MDCK and HeLa cells was performed using Calcein AM and PI staining for cells under CLSM. The confocal images of the cells cultured on the coated surface displayed a green dye color that confirmed the survival of the cells (Fig. 6 d). Additionally, to evaluate the biocompatibility and toxicity of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ antifouling behavior by visible light exposure towards MDCK and HeLa cells, an MTT assay was performed. Based on the results shown in Fig. 6 e, the cell viability of MDCK and HeLa cells was maintained at almost 100% before and after 3 h of visible light irradiation with a coating concentration of 5 mg/mL.

We fabricated a CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surface as an electrochemical biosensor to rapidly and sensitively detect cancer cells based on the response of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ to distinctive intracellular ALP and PPi concentrations between cancer and normal cells with antifouling effects in the presence of visible light. The electrochemical signals, including EIS and the wireless system, changed in cancer and normal cells cultured on CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+-coated surfaces, indicating the distinctive responsiveness of the interaction between Cu2+ and CD-coated biosensors under the dephosphorylation of PPi by ALP. The cancer cell-treated biosensors significantly increased the resistance with respect to the amount of Cu2+ that was taken up by the chelation interaction with PPi in cancer cells and cellular internalization of CD(HA) due to the time-dependent detachment of cancer cells by visible light irradiation. In contrast, no remarkable changes were observed after the detachment of normal cells, owing to the lower concentration of intracellular PPi-ALP in the microenvironment, which resulted in no uptake in normal cells. CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ was shown to be a great material for cell-surface investigation, altering the electrochemical signals of CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors obtained by electrochemical instruments in response to the ALP and PPi-responsive normal and cancer cellular microenvironment, facilitated by the antifouling effect in accordance with visible light, with harmless impacts on cell viability. Therefore, the CD(HA)/TiO2/Cu2+ biosensors exhibited a promising potential in future clinical work which could be applied in cancer patients as it elucidated a supportive and favorable approach for the detection of cancer, especially at the early stage of tumor growth.
